title: American Express Under Squeri: Playing Offense During Crisis
content:
When Stephen Squeri took charge of American Express in 2018, he immediately pledged a 'revenue first' approach that prioritized top-line expansion. He significantly ramped up the marketing budget to attract a younger, more affluent demographic to the brand's premium cards. Platinum cardholders received new lifestyle benefits, and the company heavily invested in expanding its exclusive airport lounge network. Furthermore, Squeri oversaw the strategic purchase of the restaurant reservation platform Resy to integrate highly sought-after dining perks. Despite these successful updates, the company's core product remained fundamentally tethered to a wide menu of travel benefits.

This travel-heavy strategy faced a dire scenario when the global COVID-19 pandemic struck and effectively halted international movement. The company's premium products, which were rich in travel-focused benefits, literally became irrelevant overnight. While top management debated a hunkered-down, cost-flattening campaign that included widespread layoffs, Squeri boldly decided to play offense. He chose to invest heavily in customers by introducing temporary credits for streaming services and cell phone plans that were useful during lockdowns. He also adamantly refused to lay off any employees, believing that retaining trained staff was crucial for post-pandemic recovery.

This aggressive investment strategy initially risked generating an immense financial loss for the company during the crisis. However, Squeri secured the backing of major shareholders by arguing that protecting the brand's aura and customer loyalty was paramount. Ultimately, the new suite of stay-at-home benefits successfully prevented mass customer defections while government programs stabilized the broader economy. When the crisis lifted, Amex kept all the added perks and integrated even more lifestyle partnerships to boost the brand's everyday appeal. Today, the company's revenues have vastly exceeded pre-pandemic levels, proving that investing in customer value during a crisis can yield massive long-term dividends.
